channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Word of the Day: penury
item.description: This word has appeared in nine articles on NYTimes.com in the past year. Can you use it in a sentence?
item.dc:creator: The Learning Network

item.title: SpudCell, la célula sintética que podría cambiar la investigación médica
item.description: SpudCell no está viva en sentido estricto, pero reproduce varias funciones esenciales y abre una nueva etapa en la biología sintética.
item.dc:creator: K. R. Callaway

item.title: El verdadero significado del sionismo
item.description: El mal uso de la palabra ha simplificado en exceso su complejidad.
item.dc:creator: Ari D. Berman

media:content.media:credit: Mauricio Lima para The New York Times

item.title: La bandera de Inglaterra, entre el fútbol y la política
item.description: Mientras avanza la Copa del Mundo, la cruz de San Jorge vuelve a ondear en casas y calles, pero para muchos ya no representa lo mismo.
item.dc:creator: Megan Specia

item.title: It’s Too Hot to Cook. But Food Truck Workers Have No Choice.
item.description: The heat wave in New York this week is even hotter for those working, often without air-conditioning, in the carts that line city blocks.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy

media:content.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: For the many food truck workers that serve meals around the city, air-conditioning is usually nonexistent.

item.title: A Parade of Tall Ships Is Heading to New York. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: The Hudson River will be filled with dozens of tall ships from at least 20 nations this Fourth of July for Sail4th 250.
item.dc:creator: Claire Fahy
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces

media:content.media:credit: Mingson Lau/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: The ships and boats involved with the Sail4th 250 event on Fourth of July weekend will carry roughly 20,000 sailors.

item.title: NYT Spelling Bee Answers for July 2, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: Teenage Cadets Hospitalized in New York State After Bus Air Conditioning Fails
item.description: The authorities said the cadets — it wasn’t clear how many — were removed from a bus in New York State’s Hudson Valley after suffering heat-related illnesses.
item.dc:creator: John Yoon

item.title: Estos son algunos detalles de la boda de Taylor Swift en el Madison Square Garden
item.description: Según un memorándum interno de la policía de Nueva York, el evento comenzará a las 5:30 p. m. y terminará cerca de las 2 a. m.
item.dc:creator: Maria Cramer, Chelsia Rose Marcius and Emma G. Fitzsimmons

media:content.media:credit: Frank Franklin II/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Se espera que los planes de Taylor Swift y Travis Kelce requieran la presencia de cientos de agentes de policía.

item.title: Los ingresos de Trump durante su presidencia no tienen precedente histórico
item.description: La decisión del presidente de poner en marcha nuevos proyectos empresariales, en lugar de eliminar posibles conflictos de intereses, va en contra de una tradición muy arraigada.
item.dc:creator: Eric Lipton

media:content.media:credit: Pete Marovich para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: La mayor parte de los nuevos ingresos del presidente Trump proviene del sector de las criptomonedas, al que su familia se lanzó con fuerza a partir de finales de 2024.

item.title: Chanel Is Buying Charvet, the World’s Oldest Shirt Maker
item.description: The French luxury group is acquiring Charvet, a Parisian brand that dates to the 1830s, in a bid to add some men’s wear savoir-faire to its portfolio.
item.dc:creator: Vanessa Friedman

media:content.media:credit: Randy Holmes/Disney, via Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Jacob Elordi wearing a Chanel x Charvet shirt for a January talk show appearance. According to Chanel’s president of fashion, Charvet’s brother-and-sister owners began mulling a sale after the success of that collaboration.

item.title: The World Has Failed the Uyghurs
item.description: If the global community can’t stop China’s atrocities against the Uyghurs, it must help us keep our culture alive in exile.
item.dc:creator: Tahir Imin
item.category: War Crimes, Genocide and Crimes Against Humanity

item.title: Cómo la guerra en Irán enfrentó a Trump y el príncipe heredero saudita
item.description: Mohammed bin Salman había presionado al presidente Trump para que debilitara a Irán. Pero a medida que Irán iba demostrando su poder, el príncipe instó a un alto al fuego y ahora se enfoca en sus prioridades de seguridad.
item.dc:creator: Edward Wong
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: El presidente Trump con el príncipe heredero Mohammed bin Salman de Arabia Saudita en Washington el año pasado. Los dos países están cada vez más enfrentados por la guerra de EE. UU. contra Irán.

item.title: Kyiv Takes Shelter From Overnight Strikes by Russia
item.description: Residents of the Ukrainian capital of Kyiv came prepared to spend the night underground as Russia fired attack drones and ballistic missiles.
item.dc:creator: Shawn Paik and Cassandra Vinograd

media:content.media:credit: Gleb Garanich/Reuters
media:content.media:description: Smoke rising after a Russian strike in Kyiv, Ukraine, on Thursday.

item.title: U.S. Resumes Dollar Transfers to Iraq After Monthslong Suspension
item.description: The Trump administration had halted the shipments to Iraq as part of its efforts to pressure the Baghdad government to distance itself from Iran.
item.dc:creator: Raja Abdulrahim and Falih Hassan

media:content.media:credit: Hadi Mizban/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Iraq’s prime minister, Ali al-Zaidi, has taken steps to try to rein in Iran-backed militias in his country.

item.title: Quote of the Day: Legal Scholars Surprised by Justices’ Close Ruling on Birthright Citizenship
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Thursday, July 2, 2026.

item.title: Can the World’s Largest Stadium Explain the Global Economy?
item.description: High growth rates and surging exports in Vietnam mask deep frustrations in one of Asia’s most ambitious countries.
item.dc:creator: Damien Cave and Linh Pham
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:description: Workers outside the construction site of Hung Vuong Stadium in Hanoi, Vietnam, in May.

item.title: Can Companies Embrace A.I. Without Layoffs? This One Says It Is Trying to.
item.description: The German software giant SAP says it is betting that employees can reinvent jobs instead of eliminating them. Experts are divided on whether it will work.
item.dc:creator: Jim Tankersley

media:content.media:credit: Lena Mucha for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Matthias Deindl, a product management executive for the company SAP, at an A.I.-enhanced production line in Walldorf, Germany.

item.title: Man Charged With Killing Postal Worker on Her Route in North Carolina
item.description: William Craig Durham, 56, faces murder and kidnapping charges in the death of Brandi Reynolds, 35, the authorities say.
item.dc:creator: Mack Liederman

media:content.media:credit: Benoit Tessier/Reuters
media:content.media:description: Ms. Reynolds had been delivering mail at the time of her death, said an inspector for the United States Postal Inspection Service.

item.title: NYT Crossword Answers for July 02, 2026
item.description: David J. Kahn takes us back to history class.
item.dc:creator: Sean McGowan

media:content.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: If you were hoping for a puzzle that’s straight over the plate, I’m sorry to burst your bubble. I am also sorry about those puns.

item.title: Maps: Tracking Tropical Storm Bavi
item.description: See the likely path and wind arrival times for Bavi
item.dc:creator: William B. Davis, Madison Dong, Judson Jones, John Keefe, Joey K. Lee and Bea Malsky

media:content.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: How a Nation of Immigrants Traces Its Roots
item.description: Melting pot, tapestry, mosaic, kaleidoscope, salad bowl. Every cliché is true.
item.dc:creator: Albert Sun, Jeff Adelson and Larry Buchanan

media:content.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: For Congo Fans, the Thrill of Victory Was So Close, Until It Wasn’t
item.description: Congo lost, 2-1, to England on Wednesday, after spending most of the game winning.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean

media:content.media:credit: Finbarr O'Reilly for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Congolese fans in the capital, Kinshasa, react as England scored a winning goal during the second half of the game on Wednesday. After leading for much of the match, Congo lost, 2-1.

item.title: What America’s 250th Means to Mamdani
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani has not actively pushed New York City’s role in the nation’s anniversary celebrations, in part because of the complexities he sees in the country’s past and present.
item.dc:creator: Emma Goldberg

media:content.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani has often seemed most comfortable expressing a sort of New York City-focused patriotism, rather than one rooted in the nation’s history.

item.title: Immigrant Arrests Surge to 10,000 in 5 Days as ICE Clamps Down
item.description: The agency has doubled its daily arrest numbers without the fanfare of last year’s large urban operations, sowing fear in immigrant communities.
item.dc:creator: Hamed Aleaziz

media:content.media:credit: Michael M. Santiago/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Immigration and Customs Enforcement agents patrolling a federal court building in New York last month.

item.title: How Trump Made ‘Y.M.C.A.’ His Anthem, Despite the Village People and Victor Willis’s Mixed Feelings
item.description: On social media, he said the Village People’s lead singer, Victor Willis, was with him “right from the beginning.” But the president has a more complicated history with the group.
item.dc:creator: Aishvarya Kavi

media:content.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President-elect Donald J. Trump danced to “Y.M.C.A.” along with the Village People during a pre-inauguration event in January last year.

item.title: Russia Strikes Ukraine as Explosions Rock Capital of Kyiv
item.description: At least 13 people were killed in the assault, which began hours after President Volodymyr Zelensky of Ukraine warned that Russia was preparing another “massive strike” on Kyiv.
item.dc:creator: Cassandra Vinograd

media:content.media:credit: Valentyn Ogirenko/Reuters
media:content.media:description: Rescuers on Thursday at an apartment building in Kyiv that was damaged in overnight Russian strikes.

item.title: Federal Inmate Charged With Lending Contraband Phone to Sex Trafficker
item.description: David Motovich, an inmate at a Brooklyn detention center, was said to have lent an illegal cellphone to Oren Alexander, who was convicted of sex crimes along with his brothers earlier this year.
item.dc:creator: Debra Kamin and Santul Nerkar

media:content.media:credit: Brendan Mcdermid/Reuters
media:content.media:description: Already convicted of financial crimes, David Motovich illegally obtained and lent out a cellphone while awaiting sentencing, federal prosecutors said.

item.title: Nine Arrested in Federal Crackdown on L.A.’s Sex-Trafficking Corridor
item.description: Federal officials in Los Angeles described gang members’ yearslong scheme that relied on social media and physical violence to recruit and maintain control of the girls.
item.dc:creator: Emily Baumgaertner Nunn

item.title: Bureau of Prisons Will Close Facilities Housing Thousands of Inmates
item.description: The agency pointed to crumbling infrastructure, chronic staffing shortages and budget shortfalls as it moved to cut costs.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron

media:content.media:credit: Julia Robinson for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The federal prison in Big Spring, Texas, is among the facilities to close.

item.title: Ruling on Trans Athletes Gave the G.O.P. a Win. Most Democrats Looked the Other Way.
item.description: While Republicans celebrated the ruling, many Democrats stayed quiet on an issue that had proved divisive in the last election.
item.dc:creator: Emily Davies and Tim Balk

media:content.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Republicans celebrated the Supreme Court’s ruling this week upholding laws banning transgender athletes from competing in women’s sports. Democrats were largely silent on the matter.

item.title: Trump Administration Delivers Lucrative Win for Its Kratom Allies
item.description: In moving to ban a potent synthetic version of kratom, the president’s team paved the way for more sales for makers of rival botanic supplements, who had aggressively lobbied for the change.
item.dc:creator: Christina Jewett and Kenneth P. Vogel

media:content.media:credit: Nick Oxford for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: After President Trump’s victory, companies selling kratom supplements and their executives ramped up their spending on lobbyists with ties to his administration and donations to his political operation.

item.title: Trump Finds His Presidential Alter Ego, the ‘He-Man’ Teddy Roosevelt
item.description: President Trump flew into town on the new Air Force One and spent time touring the library dedicated to Theodore Roosevelt, whom he called “a great he-man.”
item.dc:creator: Shawn McCreesh

media:content.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump arrived on a train at the Theodore Roosevelt Presidential Library in North Dakota on Wednesday.

item.title: ¿Y si sí? México encuentra esperanza en la Copa Mundial
item.description: El desempeño de la selección mexicana en el Mundial ha electrizado a la nación, atrayendo a cientos de miles de personas a celebraciones callejeras que se han convertido en expresión de resiliencia y optimismo colectivos.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas and Luis Antonio Rojas

media:content.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times

item.title: Who is Ahead in Texas's 28th Congressional District?
item.description: Track the latest polls in Texas's 28th Congressional District.

item.title: Who is Ahead in Arizona's 4th Congressional District?
item.description: Track the latest polls in Arizona's 4th Congressional District.

item.title: 5 Songs to Get You Moving This Summer
item.description: Summer has officially begun, and it’s time to be out dancing. From K-pop to house to electro, our critic Jon Caramanica has five songs to get you started.
item.dc:creator: Jon Caramanica, Arjun Srivatsa and Joe Coscarelli

item.title: Does Trump Worry About Conflicts of Interest? ‘I Found Out That Nobody Cared.’
item.description: American presidents have generally tried to avoid appearing to profit from the office. President Trump has chosen a different path.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs

media:content.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump, followed by his sons Donald Jr. and Eric, steps off the new Air Force One in Bismarck, N.D., on Wednesday.

item.title: The Key Ways Trump’s Financial Interests Intersect With Government Policy
item.description: President Trump’s business holdings, which garnered him more than $2 billion last year, create potential conflicts of interest surpassing any predecessor.
item.dc:creator: Ben Protess, Andrea Fuller and Eric Lipton

media:content.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: President Trump’s financial investments have vastly escalated during this second term.

item.title: Colorado Governor Fires Officials Who Opposed Freeing Tina Peters
item.description: Gov. Jared Polis commuted the sentence of Tina Peters, who tampered with voting machines in an attempt to show that the 2020 election had been rigged against President Trump.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy

media:content.media:credit: Pool photo by Hyoung Chang
media:content.media:description: Gov. Jared Polis, a Democrat under pressure from the president, asked the clemency board to reconsider the case.

item.title: Ukraine War Crosses a Grim Milestone
item.description: Also, scientists create a cell with the hallmarks of life. Here’s the latest at the end of Wednesday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen

media:content.media:credit: Nicole Tung for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The coffin of Vasyl Ratushnyi, 28, in Kyiv last year.

item.title: America Rings In 250th Birthday Amid Deep Polarization and Political Rifts
item.description: How does a deeply polarized country come together to have a birthday party?
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei

media:content.media:credit: Alex Kent/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The festivities at the Great American State Fair on the National Mall.

item.title: Judge Blocks Postal Service From Imposing Restrictions on Mail-In Ballots
item.description: The ruling, based on agreements the service made in a 2021 legal settlement, was the second time a judge has blocked new curbs by President Trump on voting by mail.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague and Adam Sella
item.category: National Assn for the Advancement of Colored People

media:content.media:credit: Philip Cheung for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Mail-in ballots being sorted in City of Industry, Calif., last year.

item.title: Promising Much to Many, Johnson Loses His Grip on the House
item.description: Republicans are growing frustrated with Mr. Johnson’s approach to governing with a razor-thin majority, saying that he promises more than he can achieve, frustrating the disparate groups in his caucus.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold and Carl Hulse

media:content.media:credit: Demetrius Freeman for The New York Times

item.title: August Trial Date Is Set for Brooklyn Power Broker Accused of Bribery
item.description: Lawyers for Frank Carone, who was an aide to former Mayor Eric Adams, said they want his trial to begin as soon as possible.
item.dc:creator: Santul Nerkar

media:content.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Frank Carone, a top aide to former Mayor Eric Adams, was accused of taking bribes in exchange for steering a contract to a developer.

item.title: ¿Y Si Sí? The World Cup Delivers Hope to Mexico
item.description: Mexico’s World Cup run has electrified the nation, drawing hundreds of thousands into the streets in celebrations of collective joy, resilience and hope.
item.dc:creator: Paulina Villegas and Luis Antonio Rojas

media:content.media:credit: Luis Antonio Rojas for The New York Times

item.title: World Cup Success Is Giving Americans ‘Permission to Be Patriotic’
item.description: Heading into the World Cup, many Americans had mixed feelings about the tournament and their nation.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango
item.category: United States Men's National Soccer Team

media:content.media:credit: Gabriela Bhaskar/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Many American fans have been feeling better about their country after the success of their team and positive reviews of Americana from foreign visitors.

item.title: An American Mosaic
item.description: This map shows how people in the United States identify their ancestry or family origin. Explore the many ways we describe our heritage and ourselves.
item.dc:creator: Albert Sun, Jeff Adelson and Larry Buchanan

media:content.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: U.S.A.I.D. Cuts Killed People. That’s the Truth.
item.description: Musk insists that his demolition of humanitarian aid didn’t cost lives. So I propose he take a trip with me.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Kristof
item.category: President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
United States Agency for International Development

media:content.media:credit: Saidu Bah for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Moriba Freeman’s pregnant daughter, Yamah Freeman, in yellow, bled to death en route to a hospital.

item.title: ¿Qué hace Venezuela con sus muertos?
item.description: A medida que aumenta el número de fallecidos, el sistema forense de Venezuela se ha visto desbordado con miles de cuerpos, hasta el punto de que un puerto se ha convertido en una morgue provisional.
item.dc:creator: Tibisay Romero, Isayen Herrera, Luis Ferré-Sadurní and Genevieve Glatsky

media:content.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The authorities have started an improvised morgue, so that relatives can look for the bodies of loved ones among the earthquake’s victims at La Guaira’s port in Venezuela.
